title: Shorea sumatrana
intro: Shorea sumatrana, commonly known as the Sumatran mahogany, is a species of tree in the family Dipterocarpaceae. The bark of Shorea sumatrana has been traditionally used in medicine, exhibiting potential health benefits such as anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antiviral properties, which may aid in the treatment of various diseases. The bioactive constituents of this plant include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Shorea sumatrana include infusions, decoctions, and tinctures, which are often used in traditional medicine to treat various health issues.
taxonomy: Shorea sumatrana is classified in the family Dipterocarpaceae. This species belongs to the genus Shorea. Shorea sumatrana is further classified as a species of flowering plant native to Indonesia. It is a type of dipterocarp tree that is commonly found in the tropical rainforests of Sumatra. The classification of Shorea sumatrana is also supported by its unique leaf and seed characteristics.
uses: Shorea sumatrana is known for its potential medicinal uses, including its antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. The plant's extracts have been studied for their ability to inhibit the growth of certain bacteria and fungi. Additionally, Shorea sumatrana has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health issues, including fever and respiratory problems. Further research is needed to fully understand the plant's medicinal properties and potential applications. More studies are required to confirm its effectiveness in treating various health conditions.

preparations: Shorea sumatrana, also known as the Kapok or Ramin tree, is a valuable resource for herbal preparations in traditional medicine. The bark and leaves of the tree are used to make remedies for various health issues, including fever, skin conditions, and digestive problems. In some cultures, the tree's sap is used to treat wounds and reduce inflammation.
